content:
11 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I’m a full-stack engineer who works primarily with JavaScript and Node.js. For years, I kept hearing the same things: “Rust is blazing fast,” “C++ gives you control,” “Java and JS have overhead because of garbage collection.”
I nodded along. I kind of got it. But I never really understood it. What overhead? Where? What exactly is sitting in my machine’s memory when I run a Node server versus a Rust server? Where does the garbage collector live? If Rust has a compiler and JS also has a compiler, what’s actually different?
Then I spent a few hours pulling on these threads, and everything clicked. If you’ve ever had the same fuzzy understanding, this article is for you.

Let’s Start With What Your Computer Actually Does
Before we talk about languages, we need to agree on one thing: your CPU only understands machine code. That’s it. Binary instructions like 0x48 0x89 0xe5. It doesn't know what a for loop is. It doesn't know what an object is. It doesn't know JavaScript or Rust or Python.
Every programming language, no matter how high-level, has to eventually get turned into these machine instructions. The way it gets turned into machine instructions — and when — is the fundamental difference that separates languages into two camps.

Camp 1: Ahead-of-Time Compilation (Rust, C, C++, Go)
You write your code. You run a compiler. The compiler reads your source code, analyzes it, optimizes it, and spits out a binary file — a file full of machine code that your CPU can execute directly.
Here’s the important part: the compiler then exits. It’s done. It’s not running anymore. It’s not in memory. The binary it produced is a self-contained, standalone program. When you run that binary, it loads into RAM and talks directly to the CPU. There is no middleman.
The compiler is like a translator who converts an English book into Hindi, hands you the Hindi book, and walks away. You can read the Hindi book forever. The translator is not sitting next to you while you read.
Camp 2: Just-in-Time Compilation with a Runtime (JavaScript, Java, Python)
You write your code. You run a command like node app.js. But here, something very different happens. A runtime environment loads into your machine's RAM — and it stays there for the entire lifetime of your program.
For JavaScript, that runtime is Node.js (or the browser). For Java, it’s the JVM. This runtime is a large, complex program written in C/C++ that does everything your code needs to function: it reads your source code, compiles it on the fly, manages memory, runs a garbage collector, and provides access to system resources like the filesystem and network.
The runtime is like a simultaneous interpreter sitting next to you in a meeting, translating every sentence as it’s spoken. If the interpreter leaves, you can’t understand anything anymore. The interpreter must stay for the entire meeting.

OK, But Let’s Get Concrete. What Exactly Happens When I Run node app.js?
Let’s say you have a file called app.js on your disk:
let users = [
{ name: "Alice", age: 30 },
{ name: "Bob", age: 25 }
];
users.push({ name: "Charlie", age: 35 });
console.log(users);Right now, this is just a text file. Characters on a disk. It means absolutely nothing to your CPU.
You open your terminal and type node app.js. Here's what happens, step by step:
Step 1: The OS loads the Node binary into RAM.
When you installed Node on your machine, it placed an executable file at a path like /usr/bin/node. This file is a compiled C++ program — the result of compiling the entire Node.js project (which includes V8, libuv, and a bunch of C++ bindings) into one executable. This single binary file is typically around 50–80MB.
The operating system reads this file from disk and loads it into RAM. Now V8 (the JavaScript engine), libuv (the event loop), and the garbage collector are all sitting in your machine’s memory as compiled machine code, ready to execute.
Step 2: V8 reads your JavaScript file.
V8 reads the text of app.js from disk into memory. At this point it's still just a string of characters. V8 parses this string into an internal data structure called an AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) — a tree representation of your program's structure.
Step 3: V8 compiles your code to machine code — in memory.
V8’s JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler takes the AST and generates actual CPU instructions from it. But unlike Rust’s compiler, V8 doesn’t write these instructions to a file on disk. The machine code is generated directly in RAM. There is no intermediate binary file.
Step 4: The CPU executes those instructions.
When it hits let users = [...], V8's memory allocator (C++ code) asks the OS for a chunk of RAM and stores the array objects there. V8 keeps track of every allocation internally.
Step 5: The garbage collector does its thing.
The GC is C++ code that’s part of V8, sitting in RAM inside the same Node process as your code. Periodically, it wakes up, scans through all the objects V8 has allocated, figures out which ones are no longer reachable from your code, and frees that memory. While the GC is scanning, your JavaScript code is paused. This is the infamous “GC pause” or “stop-the-world” pause that people talk about.
Step 6: The event loop keeps everything alive.
libuv (a C library, part of the Node binary) is running an infinite loop: “Are there pending I/O callbacks? Timers? Network events?” As long as there’s something to wait for (like an HTTP server listening for requests), Node keeps running. The moment there’s nothing left, the process exits.
So what’s sitting in RAM while your Node server runs?

• The entire V8 engine (parser, JIT compiler, garbage collector, memory manager) — compiled C++ machine code

• libuv (the event loop) — compiled C code

• The machine code that V8 generated from your JavaScript — sitting in RAM, no file on disk

• All the JavaScript objects your code created (the users array, the objects inside it) — on V8's managed heap in RAM

• V8’s internal bookkeeping structures (object maps, type feedback, optimization metadata)
All of this together easily starts at 50–100MB of RAM before your application logic has done anything meaningful. For a simple Express.js “Hello World” server, you’re already using 30–50MB just to keep the runtime alive.

Now Let’s Do the Same Thing With Rust
You have a file called main.rs:
use std::net::TcpListener;fn main() {
let listener = TcpListener::bind("0.0.0.0:8080").unwrap();
for stream in listener.incoming() {
// handle each connection
}
}Again, just text on disk. Here’s what happens — but notice how different the process is.
Step 1: You compile on your own machine (or in CI).
You run cargo build --release. The OS loads the Rust compiler (rustc) into RAM. rustc reads your source code, parses it, runs type checking, runs the borrow checker (Rust's ownership rules are enforced here), and hands everything off to LLVM (a compiler backend written in C++).
LLVM optimizes the code and generates a binary file — let’s call it my_server. This file is written to disk. It's maybe 5–10MB. It contains raw machine code.
And then the compiler exits. The rustc process is gone. It's not in memory anymore. Its job is done.
Step 2: You deploy just the binary.
You copy my_server to your server (an EC2 instance, a Docker container, whatever). Note: you don't need to install Rust on the server. You don't need rustc. You don't need Cargo. The binary is completely self-contained.
Step 3: You run the binary.
You run ./my_server. The OS loads the binary into RAM. The CPU starts executing the machine instructions directly. When the code creates a TcpListener, the compiled code makes a system call (socket(), bind(), listen()) directly to the OS kernel. No middleware. No runtime.
Step 4: Memory is managed deterministically — no GC.
When a value goes out of scope in Rust, the compiler has already inserted cleanup code (a drop call) at exactly the right point in the binary. There's no garbage collector scanning the heap. There's no "stop-the-world" pause. Memory is freed the instant it's no longer needed, because the compiler figured out when that would be at compile time and baked the instructions right into the binary.
So what’s sitting in RAM while your Rust server runs?

• Your compiled binary’s machine code

• Whatever memory your program explicitly allocates (connection buffers, request data, response bodies)
That’s it. No runtime engine. No JIT compiler. No garbage collector process. Maybe 5–10MB total for a basic HTTP server.

The EC2 Mental Model
This is where it all comes together. Imagine two EC2 instances in a data center, both running HTTP servers that do the same thing.
The Node.js EC2 Instance
Somewhere in a rack in us-east-1, there's a physical machine. In its RAM, the following things are loaded and running 24/7:

• The V8 JavaScript engine (~20MB of compiled C++ code)

• V8’s JIT compiler, continuously analyzing and recompiling hot code paths

• V8’s garbage collector, periodically waking up to scan the heap and free dead objects (pausing your code while it does so)

• libuv’s event loop

• Your JavaScript source code (loaded as text, compiled to machine code in memory by V8)

• All the runtime bookkeeping (type feedback vectors, hidden classes, optimization metadata)

• Your application’s actual data (request/response objects, database connections, etc.)
Total baseline memory: 50–100MB+
The Rust EC2 Instance
Same data center, same rack, different machine. In its RAM:

• Your compiled binary (~5–10MB of machine code)

• Your application’s actual data
Total baseline memory: 5–10MB
No runtime. No garbage collector. No JIT compiler. Nothing else. The machine code runs directly on the CPU, allocates memory when it needs to, frees it the instant it’s done, and never pauses for anything.
This is why when Discord rewrote their Read States service from Go to Rust, their tail latencies dropped dramatically — Go’s garbage collector was causing periodic latency spikes. The Rust version had completely predictable, flat latency because there’s no GC to interrupt execution.
This is why Cloudflare runs Rust at the edge for their Workers runtime — when you’re processing millions of requests per second, every megabyte of RAM overhead per process matters.

“But Wait — JS Also Has a Compiler, and Rust Also Has a Compiler. So What’s Different?”
This was the question that confused me the most. Let me make it razor sharp.
Rust’s compiler (rustc)

• Runs before your program executes

• Reads source code → produces a binary file on disk → exits

• Is not present at runtime

• Does its work once, ahead of time
This is called Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compilation.
V8’s compiler (inside Node.js)

• Runs during your program’s execution

• Reads source code → produces machine code in RAM (no file on disk) → stays alive

• Might recompile functions multiple times as it learns about types and hot paths

• Is just one component of a larger runtime that must remain in memory
This is called Just-in-Time (JIT) compilation.
The word “compiler” is doing very different jobs in these two contexts. Rust’s compiler is a standalone tool that produces a finished product. V8’s compiler is an internal organ of a living runtime that continuously does work while your program runs.
Think of it this way:
Rust’s compiler is a factory that builds a car. The car drives off the lot and drives forever. The factory doesn’t ride along inside the car.
Node/V8 is a factory that’s built into the car itself, continuously manufacturing and replacing engine parts while the car is driving down the highway. More capable in some ways, but the car is a lot heavier.

“If Rust Is So Much Better, Why Doesn’t Everyone Use It?”
Because the tradeoff is developer productivity and iteration speed.
In JavaScript, you write code, save the file, and the changes are running in seconds. The runtime figures everything out for you — memory, types, compilation. You
